macro_segments.title: Introductory Overview: Acts Warranting Lashes — Hair Removal and Flesh Modifications

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the daf and the primary topics: bald spots, sideburns, beard cutting, and flesh incisions
Definition and Talmudic interpretation of 'makif' (circling/cutting of sideburns)
Gemara discussion of 'sof rosho' (end of the head) and the specific anatomical boundaries of sideburns

macro_segments.title: Liability for Both Actor and Recipient in Hair Removal Violation

micro_segments.title: Talmudic dispute: whether both the one performing the haircut and the one receiving it incur lashes
Objection based on the principle of 'lav she'ein bo ma'aseh' (prohibition without an act) and Rabbi Yehuda's position on passive transgressions
Resolution: the plural form 'lo takifu' includes both parties; discussion of 'mesayei'a' (assisting) as active participation

macro_segments.title: Beard Cutting Prohibitions: Definition of 'Peiat HaZakan' and Method of Cutting

micro_segments.title: Anatomical definition of the five corners of the beard ('peiat hazakan') according to Talmudic sources

macro_segments.title: Flesh Incisions for the Dead: Prohibition, Multiple Acts, and Methodology

micro_segments.title: Talmudic teaching on incisions in flesh ('sherit') as a mourning-related violation restricted to mourning for the dead
Debate between Rabbi Yose and the Sages on whether one incision for multiple dead persons constitutes multiple transgressions
Distinction between 'sherita' (incision by hand) and 'gedida' (cutting with a tool); whether both terms refer to the same act
Statement of Samuel: incision with a tool ('masharey bichli') constitutes liability; relationship to pagan worship practices

macro_segments.title: Flesh Incisions in the Context of Pagan Worship and Medical Exception

micro_segments.title: Talmudic discussion of incisions in flesh as a form of pagan ritual and worship; distinction between hand and tool in idolatrous context
Rambam's perspective: many prohibitions in this passage stem from their association with pagan practices
Distinction in liability: incisions for the dead require a tool ('bichli chayav'); incisions for idolatry require a hand ('beyad chayav')

macro_segments.title: Beard Cutting Prohibitions: Definition of 'Peiat HaZakan' and Method of Cutting (continued)

micro_segments.title: Debate between Rabbi Eliezer and the Sages on whether simultaneous cutting of all corners constitutes one or multiple transgressions
Requirement of a razor ('ta'ar') versus scissors ('misparayim') and tweezers: interpretation of 'lo tashchit' (do not destroy) and 'lo yigalechu' (shall not shave)
Extended discussion of electric shavers: whether they constitute 'tashchit' (destroying to the root) and whether they are considered 'derekh giluach' (normal shaving method)

macro_segments.title: Tattooing ('Ketovot Ka'aka'): Writing and Incision as Separate Elements

micro_segments.title: Mishna requirement: both writing ('ketiva') and incision ('ke'aka') must occur to incur liability for tattooing
Debate over whether incision with ink or incision alone without ink constitutes a violation
Rabbi Shimon's view: liability only when writing God's name; Gemara's rejection and clarification regarding idolatrous context
Talmudic teaching of Rav Malkia: prohibition against rubbing ashes into wounds to avoid appearance of tattooing
Medical exception: wounds demonstrably made for healing purposes do not constitute tattooing violation

macro_segments.title: Tattooing ('Ketovot Ka'aka'): Writing and Incision as Separate Elements (continued)

micro_segments.title: Contemporary application: debate on cosmetic tattooing for breast reconstruction and whether it requires writing or merely marking

macro_segments.title: Multiple Acts and Separate Warnings: The Principle of Dividing Transgressions

micro_segments.title: Mishna on repeated violations: drinking wine throughout the day versus drinking with separate warnings between acts
Principle: continuous action without intervening warnings constitutes one transgression; separate warnings make each act distinct
Application to cemetery contamination, shaving, and wearing forbidden garments ('kilayim')
Distinction between ongoing state (remaining in violation) versus repeated actions (putting on and taking off garments)

macro_segments.title: One Furrow, Multiple Transgressions: Plowing on Shabbat and Combinations of Prohibited Acts

micro_segments.title: Mishna example: a single furrow can violate eight separate prohibitions simultaneously (bikor ox, hekdesh donkey, kilayim, shmitta, Yom Tov, kohein in cemetery, nazir in cemetery)
Chananya ben Chakinai's objection: violations unrelated to the primary act (wearing kilayim, being kohein) should not count as part of the same act
Sages' response and methodological note: no definitive halachic conclusion; the exercise demonstrates the principle of multiple violations in one act
Tosafot's alternative counting: resolving the list of eight by redefining certain violations (two types of kilayim)
